roof_system: Primary roof field appears to be a white single-ply or coated low-slope membrane roof; adjacent lower roof areas appear to be a tan low-slope membrane roof with multiple rooftop units and equipment.

possible_roof_systems.evidence: Large white, uniform reflective roof field on the main building is visually consistent with a single-ply membrane commonly used on commercial low-slope roofs.
Bright white monolithic appearance could also indicate a coated membrane surface from aerial view; exact chemistry cannot be confirmed remotely.
Tan low-slope roof section with numerous rooftop penetrations and equipment could be a darker membrane system that has weathered to a lighter tone.
Low-slope roof form supports EPDM as a general possibility, but the visible white finish is less typical for standard EPDM without coating.

roof_age_estimate: Likely older commercial roof, roughly 15-30+ years apparent by building age and roof layout; exact roof age cannot be confirmed from aerial imagery alone.

notes: Main white roof field is mostly uniform but shows scattered darker spots and a few localized discolorations that could reflect staining, prior repairs, or minor moisture-related wear.
Lower tan roof section contains many rooftop units, vents, curbs, and visible penetrations that increase flashing and leak risk.
No obvious standing water is visible in the aerial image, but minor drainage performance cannot be verified from this view.

observations: Main roof field is bright white and generally uniform, suggesting a reflective low-slope membrane or coated surface.
Several small rooftop penetrations and mechanical items are distributed across the main field.
Adjacent lower roof areas are more heavily populated with rooftop equipment and ductwork, increasing service complexity.
Localized dark spots/discoloration are visible on the white field.
No clear tree overhang or major debris accumulation is visible in the roof areas shown.
summary: Aerial imagery dated 03/11/2018 - 03/17/2018 shows a large low-slope industrial roof with a predominantly white reflective membrane on the main field and a more equipment-dense tan roof area adjacent to it. The main roof appears generally serviceable, but scattered discoloration and the presence of multiple penetrations and rooftop units increase leak and flashing risk. No obvious ponding is visible, though drainage performance cannot be verified from aerial-only imagery
recommendation: Recommend a qualified commercial roofing contractor perform an onsite inspection to confirm roof system type, condition, flashing integrity, drainage, and any moisture-related issues; moisture testing should be performed where warranted. For the apparently restorable low-slope white roof area, evaluate silicone roof restoration before a more costly overlay or replacement, provided the substrate and warranty criteria are confirmed and needed repairs/preparation are completed. A properly designed and installed silicone system can create a seamless monolithic white reflective cool-roof surface, may reduce tear-off waste, cost, and disruption, can improve UV and hail resistance, withstand ponding water when properly designed and installed, and may qualify for a manufacturer warranty of up to 20 years. If onsite findings reveal widespread moisture, failed insulation, deck deterioration, or an incompatible substrate, then overlay or full roof
